Resume deep-dive - GitHub to Bitbucket m: Planning started with an inventory of every repository, webhook, branch protection rule, and CI/CD integration pointing at GitHub, since missing one silently breaks a pipeline after cutover. Migrated repositories in batches (not a big-bang cutover), rebuilt webhook and CI/CD integrations (Jenkins/GitHub Actions equivalents) on Bitbucket before switching teams over, and ran both systems in parallel briefly so teams could validate before GitHub was decommissioned, which minimized disruption and let the migration be rolled back per-team if something broke.
Resume deep-dive - enterprise CI/CD: You: Standardized on shared, reusable pipeline templates (Jenkins shared libraries or reusable GitHub Actions workflows) that handled the common stages - build, test, security scan, deploy - with clearly defined extension points for language/framework-specific steps, so teams didn't each write CI logic from scratch. This let teams with different stacks (Java, Python, Node) use the same pipeline skeleton while customizing only the parts that genuinely needed to differ, which cut onboarding time for new services significantly and made platform-wide changes (like adding a new security scan) a one-time update instead of N team-by-team changes.
Resume deep-dive - multi-cloud IaC: You: Kept state backends separate per cloud (GCS bucket for GCP resources, S3 for AWS, Azure Storage for Azure) rather than one shared backend, since cross-cloud state coupling adds risk without real benefit, and pinned each provider version explicitly per cloud module to avoid one provider's breaking change forcing an unplanned upgrade across all three. Secrets used each cloud's native secret manager (Secret Manager, Secrets Manager, Key Vault) rather than a single cross-cloud secret store, accessed through cloud-native identity (Workload Identity, IAM roles, Managed Identity) instead of long-lived keys wherever the CI system supported it.
Resume deep-dive - DR with Kasten K10: Y: A real test means actually restoring the backup into an isolated namespace or cluster and verifying the application starts, connects to its restored storage, and serves correct data - not just confirming the backup job completed successfully, since a backup can complete without being genuinely restorable. Kasten K10 policies were tested on a schedule (not just after initial setup) against representative stateful workloads, checking both the PersistentVolume data integrity and any application-level consistency (like database transaction logs) that a pure disk snapshot might not capture correctly.
Resume deep-dive - GCP migration: You re: The riskiest part was typically data migration and cutover for stateful systems, where downtime and data consistency both matter and can't be easily rolled back. De-risked it by migrating in phases (non-critical services first to validate the pattern), running the on-prem and GCP environments in parallel with data replication before cutover, and rehearsing the cutover procedure in a non-production dry run so the actual cutover window was executing a known-good runbook rather than improvising under time pressure.
Current market 2026 - GKE Autopilot vs S: Autopilot is attractive at that scale because it removes node management entirely (no node pool sizing, patching, or capacity planning) and enforces security best practices by default, which reduces platform team toil significantly. The tradeoff is losing fine-grained control over node configuration - custom DaemonSets, specific machine types, privileged workloads, and certain node-level tuning aren't available - so the decision comes down to whether any team has a genuine requirement Autopilot can't meet; if not, Autopilot's reduced operational burden usually wins at that scale.
Current market 2026 - Workload Identity: Configure a Workload Identity Pool and provider trusting GitHub Actions' OIDC token issuer, map specific claims (repository, branch, environment) to determine which GCP service account the token can impersonate, and have the GitHub Actions workflow request a short-lived, automatically-expiring token instead of storing a service account key as a secret. This eliminates the risk of a leaked long-lived key entirely, since there's no static credential to leak - the trust relationship is verified per-request against GitHub's identity provider.
Current market 2026 - Terraform vs OpenT: Evaluate based on which Terraform Enterprise-specific features you actually depend on (Sentinel policy as code, private registry, specific enterprise support SLAs) versus what OpenTofu's open-source ecosystem now covers, and weigh the migration effort (state format compatibility is generally fine, but tooling, CI integration, and team familiarity need rework) against the licensing cost and vendor lock-in risk of staying. For a large estate, a careful pilot migration of a few non-critical modules first de-risks the decision before committing the whole estate either way.
Current market 2026 - Gateway API migrat: Run Gateway API resources alongside existing Ingress objects during migration rather than a hard cutover, migrating one service at a time and validating traffic behavior (routing, TLS, health checks) matches before decommissioning the old Ingress rule for that service. Since Gateway API is more expressive, take the opportunity to consolidate routing logic that may have been split across multiple Ingress annotations into cleaner HTTPRoute resources, and keep the migration reversible per-service until the whole fleet has been validated on Gateway API.
Current market 2026 - image signing and: Require every image built by CI to be signed with cosign (keyless signing via OIDC is the current best practice, avoiding long-lived signing keys) and attach SLSA provenance attestations describing the build process, then configure Binary Authorization policies on the GKE cluster to reject any image that isn't signed by the trusted CI identity with valid provenance. Roll this out starting in audit/dry-run mode to catch any legitimate images that would be blocked before flipping to enforcing mode, since an overly strict policy applied immediately can block legitimate deployments.
Current market 2026 - GKE Dataplane V2:: Dataplane V2 replaces the traditional iptables-based kube-proxy with an eBPF-based dataplane, which generally improves NetworkPolicy performance and scalability at high pod/service counts and adds richer built-in network observability (flow logs, per-connection visibility) without needing a separate service mesh. Operationally, expect to validate existing NetworkPolicies still behave as expected since eBPF enforcement can differ subtly from iptables in edge cases, and take advantage of the improved visibility to simplify network debugging that previously required manual tcpdump/iptables tracing.
Current market 2026 - Config Sync at sca: Structure a hierarchical repo (or ApplicationSet in ArgoCD) so common baseline policies (network policies, resource quotas, RBAC) are defined once and inherited by every cluster/namespace, with narrow, explicit overrides only where a team genuinely needs to differ from the baseline. Use Config Sync's or ArgoCD's drift detection and automatic reconciliation to catch and correct any manual changes automatically, and structure the repo so a platform team change (like a new required label) rolls out everywhere with one commit rather than dozens of manual cluster-by-cluster updates.
Current market 2026 - Docker BuildKit: H: Use `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64` to produce multi-arch images in one build invocation, and configure BuildKit's cache export/import (`--cache-to`/`--cache-from` against a registry cache) so CI runners share build cache across runs instead of starting cold every time, which significantly speeds up builds. Combine this with pinned base image digests and reproducible dependency installs so the same Dockerfile produces bit-for-bit comparable results (aside from timestamps) across different CI runners and architectures.
Current market 2026 - node auto-provisio: Standard cluster autoscaler scales existing, pre-defined node pools up and down, which requires you to have anticipated the right node pool shapes (including a GPU pool) in advance. Node auto-provisioning goes further by automatically creating new node pools with the right machine type/GPU configuration on demand based on pending pod requirements, which fits spiky, unpredictable mixed workloads better since you don't need to pre-provision every possible shape - the tradeoff is slightly less predictability over exactly which machine types will appear in the cluster, so it's worth combining with resource quotas to bound cost.
Current market 2026 - FinOps chargeback:: Enforce required labels (team, cost-center, environment) at the Terraform module level so every resource is attributed automatically at creation time rather than relying on teams to remember to tag things manually, and use GKE namespace-level cost allocation (available via GKE's built-in cost visibility features) to attribute shared cluster costs down to the namespace/team level. Start with showback (visibility only) to build trust in the attribution accuracy before moving to actual chargeback, and keep the tagging requirement enforced by policy (rejecting untagged resources) rather than a manual checklist that erodes over time.
Current market 2026 - Terraform state at: Split state into small, purpose-scoped workspaces (per project, per environment, per logical component) rather than one giant state file, so blast radius from any single apply is naturally limited and applies stay fast since each workspace has fewer resources to plan against. Run scheduled drift-detection jobs (`terraform plan` in CI on a cadence, alerting on unexpected diffs) across all workspaces so manual console changes are caught quickly, and use a consistent module structure with remote state data sources for cross-workspace references instead of manually copying values between workspaces.
